competitors.label: List Rendering
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical Vue explanation of in-place patching, node identity, and why keyed lists matter.
theirStrengths: The official docs explain the in-place patch strategy clearly and state when keyed identity is required.
theirGaps: The page is lighter on concrete debugging symptoms such as focus jumps, wrong input values, and child-state leakage after reordering.

realWorldUseCases: Typical real bugs when keys are missing or unstable: Inputs show the wrong value after sorting/filtering because the DOM input element is reused for a different item.
actionableExamples: Index as key: when it’s wrong vs acceptable :key="index" is effectively “no stable identity” when the list can reorder, filter, insert, or delete.

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain that unkeyed lists are only suitable when output does not rely on child component state or temporary DOM state such as form input values.
actionableExamples: They show the keyed v-for pattern and explain that Vue reuses and reorders existing elements when identity is provided.
followUpConfusion: The follow-up coverage is strong on the identity rule, but it is less explicit about transition glitches, focus loss, and when index keys are only narrowly acceptable.
Keep one shorter note on why transition-group animations also depend on stable keys.

competitors.winReason: The official guide is canonical, but our page turns the identity rule into more vivid production bugs and adds a sharper index-key debugging explanation.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because it covers reactivity, bindings, lifecycle, and interactive Vue component questions.
theirStrengths: It is a strong interview-prep hub with detailed answers, solutions, and Vue-specific practice coverage.
theirGaps: As a broad question bank, it is less focused on one exact list-identity bug pattern than a dedicated answer page.

realWorldUseCases: Checkboxes/toggles flip on the wrong row (DOM state vs data association breaks).
followUpConfusion: It’s only acceptable when the list is truly static (never changes order/length) or when each row has no meaningful state (rare in real apps).

realWorldUseCases: The hub covers Vue interview topics broadly, but it does not go as deep into keyed-list state corruption on this landing page.
actionableExamples: It promises detailed answers and worked solutions, so the example bar remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less explicit about the index-key trap and identity-versus-position nuance that usually appears as the follow-up.

actionableExamples: Keep one even shorter reverse-list snippet that demonstrates the wrong-input-value bug in a single glance.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is strong as an interview hub, but our page is more specific about what actually breaks when keys are unstable and why index keys corrupt identity.

competitors.label: Vue for Loop Tips
competitors.selectionReason: LearnVue has a focused article on v-for keys, in-place patching, and why index keys behave like missing identity.
theirStrengths: It directly explains in-place patching and uses reorder plus input examples to show why keys matter.
theirGaps: It is lighter on transition-group behavior, focus bugs, and the full debugging checklist around state leakage.

realWorldUseCases: Child component local state leaks (e.g., expanded/collapsed, internal caches, timers) to a different item after reordering.
followUpConfusion: Keys aren’t about “performance only”. They’re primarily about correctness : ensuring DOM nodes and component instances stay attached to the same logical data item across updates.

realWorldUseCases: The article already uses reordered products and stateful inputs to make the bug concrete.
actionableExamples: Its before-and-after keyed reorder example is strong, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: It explains why index keys act like missing keys, but it is less explicit about when static lists are the narrow exception and how child instances leak state.

actionableExamples: Keep one short child-component example, not only a raw input example, so the state-leak story stays concrete.

competitors.winReason: LearnVue is already strong on reorder examples, but our page is broader on the real debugging fallout and clearer on the identity-first mental model.
